claim: Agents are models using tools in a loop; only use agents for complex, valuable, high-leverage tasks—not for simple or low-value workflows
actionable: Before building an agent, ask: Is this task complex and unclear how to solve? Is it high-value? Can I give the agent the tools it needs? If any answer is no, use a simpler approach
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Think like your agents: simulate being the agent given its tool descriptions and schemas to catch confusing or incomplete tool design before deployment
actionable: Walk through agent decision-making yourself: given the tools and prompts, would you know what to do? If you're confused, the agent will be too—iterate on tool design
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Give agents explicit heuristics like 'stop searching when you find the answer' and tool call budgets (5 for simple tasks, 15 for complex); they don't infer these without prompting
actionable: In agent system prompts, explicitly state stopping conditions and resource budgets; treat the agent like a junior intern and articulate practices they won't assume
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Tool selection requires explicit prompting on which tools to use for which tasks; don't assume the model knows which tool is right for your domain context
actionable: Include in system prompt: 'For company queries, search Slack first; for codebase queries, use GitHub search.' Guide tool selection per context rather than letting model guess
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Guide thinking process in agents by prompting them to plan searches in advance and use interleaved thinking to verify web search quality
actionable: Prompt agents to plan their search strategy before executing and reflect on result quality between tool calls; this improves accuracy and reduces hallucination
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Agent prompts have unintended side effects; 'keep searching until perfect' may cause infinite loops if perfect answer doesn't exist—always include fallback conditions
actionable: When prompting agents to search or iterate, pair stopping conditions with maximum effort limits; e.g., 'Find the best source, but stop after 5 attempts if none are satisfactory'
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Use context compression (compaction) to summarize conversation history when nearing token limits, allowing agents to run effectively with 200k token window
actionable: Implement a compaction tool that triggers near context limit (190k tokens) to compress conversation to a dense summary, preserving important details in a new context
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Use small manual evals starting out; don't wait for perfect automated evals with hundreds of test cases before iterating on prompts
actionable: Create 5-10 realistic test cases manually, run them against your agent, and iterate prompts based on failures—skip large automated eval infrastructure initially
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Use LLM-as-judge with clear rubrics for agent evals; it handles diverse output formats better than keyword matching
actionable: Write eval rubrics describing correctness criteria (e.g., 'answer should be 30k-50k bananas'), then use Claude to judge agent outputs against the rubric
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Design tools with simple, clear names and accurate descriptions; give agents similar tools with distinct names and don't overwhelm with 6+ nearly-identical search tools
actionable: Keep tool schemas distinct and consolidated; avoid 'search_db1', 'search_db2', 'search_db3' clutter—combine into one flexible tool with parameters
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude

claim: Start with bare-bones prompts and tools; Claude often works well out-of-the-box, so don't over-engineer upfront
actionable: Build agents with minimal prompting first, test them, identify specific failures, then add complexity; avoid premature over-specification
source_title: Prompting for Agents | Code w/ Claude
